title: Eggs and cardiovascular risk: does moderate egg consumption raise heart disease risk?
question: Does moderate egg consumption meaningfully raise cardiovascular disease risk, or is it safe for most people?

sourceDocument.title: Eggs (Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health, The Nutrition Source)
sourceDocument.text:
[Skip to content](https://nutritionsource.hsph.harvard.edu/food-features/eggs/#site-content)

[The Nutrition Source](https://nutritionsource.hsph.harvard.edu/) Menu

![Man holding basket of eggs with chickens in the background](https://nutritionsource.hsph.harvard.edu/wp-content/uploads/2024/11/AdobeStock_434804432-1024x379.jpeg)

Long-vilified for their high cholesterol content by well-meaning doctors and scientists researching heart disease, eggs now seem to be making a bit of a comeback. So what changed?

While it’s true that just one large egg yolk has 200 mg of cholesterol—making it one of the richest sources of dietary cholesterol—eggs also contain additional nutrients that may help lower the risk for heart disease. In addition, the moderate amount of fat in an egg, about 5 grams, is mostly [monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat](https://nutritionsource.hsph.harvard.edu/types-of-fat/). It’s also crucial to distinguish between _dietary cholesterol_ and _cholesterol in the blood_, [which are only weakly related](https://nutritionsource.hsph.harvard.edu/cholesterol/). The focus on dietary cholesterol alone was de-emphasized as more attention was placed on the influence of saturated and trans fat on blood cholesterol. Accordingly, the _Dietary Guidelines for Americans 2015_ removed the prior recommendation to limit consumption of dietary cholesterol to 300 mg per day. \[1\]

Given their history, “are eggs healthy?” has become a frequently asked nutrition question. To answer this, it’s important to look at eggs not only on their own, but in context of the entire diet, especially when compared to foods they may replace (and vice-versa).

## **Source Of**

- [Cholesterol](https://nutritionsource.hsph.harvard.edu/cholesterol/)
- [Protein](https://nutritionsource.hsph.harvard.edu/what-should-you-eat/protein/)
- [Choline](https://nutritionsource.hsph.harvard.edu/choline/)
- [Biotin – Vitamin B7](https://nutritionsource.hsph.harvard.edu/biotin-vitamin-b7/)
- [Vitamin A](https://nutritionsource.hsph.harvard.edu/vitamin-a/)
- Antioxidants lutein and zeaxanthin

## **Eggs and Health**

- Research on moderate egg consumption in two large prospective cohort studies (nearly 40,000 men and over 80,000 women) found that up to one egg per day is not associated with increased heart disease risk in healthy individuals. \[2\]
- Eggs were previously associated with heart disease risk as a result of their high cholesterol content. However, a solid body of research shows that for most people, cholesterol in food has a smaller effect on blood levels of total cholesterol and harmful LDL cholesterol than does the mix of fats in the diet. \[2-4\]
- ![Poached egg with runny yolk on mashed avocado and whole grain bread, with a side salad of arugula and mango red pepper salsa](https://nutritionsource.hsph.harvard.edu/wp-content/uploads/2012/09/FPG_06-EggsToastSalad-300x200.jpg)Of course, this research doesn’t give a green light to daily three-egg omelets. While a 2008 report from the Physicians’ Health Study supports the idea that eating an egg a day is generally safe for the heart, it also suggests that going much beyond that could increase the risk for heart failure later in life. \[5\] You also need to pay attention to the “trimmings” that come with your eggs. To your cardiovascular system, scrambled eggs, salsa, and a 100% whole-wheat English muffin is a far different meal than scrambled eggs with cheese, sausages, home fries, and white toast.

  - People who have difficulty controlling their total and LDL cholesterol may also want to be cautious about eating egg yolks and instead choose foods made with egg whites. The same is true for people with diabetes. In studies including the Nurses’ Health Study and Health Professionals Follow-up Study, heart disease risk was increased among men and women with diabetes who ate one or more eggs a day. \[2,4\] For people who have diabetes and heart disease, it may be best to limit egg consumption to no more than three yolks per week.
- Furthermore, to truly assess eggs and heart health, we need to examine how they stack-up to foods we might choose in their place—the classic nutrition substitution analysis. Using some common breakfast options as an example:
  - While eggs may be a much better choice than sugary, refined grain-based options like sweetened breakfast cereals, pancakes with syrup, muffins, or bagels, they may fall short of other options. A bowl of steel-cut oats with nuts and berries, for example, will be a much better choice for heart health than an egg-centric breakfast. Consumption of [whole grains](https://nutritionsource.hsph.harvard.edu/whole-grains/) and [fruit](https://nutritionsource.hsph.harvard.edu/what-should-you-eat/vegetables-and-fruits/) predict [lower risk of heart disease](https://nutritionsource.hsph.harvard.edu/disease-prevention/cardiovascular-disease/preventing-cvd/), and when it comes to protein, plant sources like nuts and seeds are related to lower cardiovascular and overall mortality, especially when compared to red meat or eggs. \[6\]
- **_The bottom line_**: While eggs may not be the optimal breakfast choice, they are certainly not the worst, falling somewhere in the middle on the spectrum of food choice and heart disease risk. For those looking to eat a healthy diet, keeping intake of eggs moderate to low will be best for most, emphasizing plant-based protein options when possible.

![Cracked egg on a table with yolk and eggshells, whisk](https://nutritionsource.hsph.harvard.edu/wp-content/uploads/2019/03/broken-cooking-crack-33819.jpg)

#### Eggs in the news

Findings from a recent study may be rekindling the debate about the role of dietary cholesterol and egg consumption in cardiovascular disease, but the results should be viewed in the context of existing research.

[A closer look at the study](https://nutritionsource.hsph.harvard.edu/2019/03/18/eggs-and-cholesterol-back-in-the-spotlight-in-new-jama-study/)

## **Storage and Food Safety**

Food safety is a top priority when handling raw eggs. A contaminated hen can transmit Salmonella inside the egg if the shell has not completely formed. Salmonella can also penetrate egg shells, which have tiny open pores.

- Look for a sell-by date on egg cartons; eggs will generally last for four to six weeks after this date. Avoid packages with cracked eggs, which can increase risk of bacterial contamination. Discard a cracked egg if you discover one after purchase.
- Promptly refrigerate eggs in their carton at 40°F or below, in the coldest part of the refrigerator. Avoid storing in side doors where the temperature varies.
- Cook eggs until the whites and yolks have solidified, to prevent food-borne illness. Egg dishes should reach an internal temperature of 160°F. If using recipes for raw or undercooked eggs, use pasteurized eggs that are heat-treated to destroy bacteria.
- Don’t allow cooked eggs or egg dishes to sit at room temperature for longer than two hours.
- Wash your hands and any surfaces with soap and water that have come in contact with raw eggs.

Free-Range? Farm-Fresh? A look at egg carton labels

When purchasing eggs, you may notice the many choices available: “free-range,” “farm-fresh,” or “vegetarian-fed.” This is a response by poultry farms to an increasing awareness on animal welfare as well as consumer health concerns. Many commercial farms raise chickens in battery cages that restrict most if not complete movement, as they are believed to be efficient for egg production. Some farms allow chickens to roam more freely and be exposed to fresh air and sunlight, prompting the new labeling terms. However, these terms are not regulated, so the amount of time and type of outdoor setting can vary depending on the farm.  Welfare-related terms you may find on an egg carton in the United States include:

- _Cage-free_ – Chickens are not confined to cages and may roam freely indoors but not necessarily outdoors.
- _Free-range/roaming_ – Chickens are allowed to roam freely outdoors but the amount of time varies and they are not necessarily given access to a pasture setting.
- _Pasture-raised_ – Chickens roam outdoors in a pasture setting and forage for food such as greens and insects, though the amount of time to roam varies.
- _Organic_ – This term is regulated by the USDA’s National Organic Program. [According to the USDA](http://blogs.usda.gov/2016/09/13/usda-graded-cage-free-eggs-all-theyre-cracked-up-to-be/), “certified organic eggs are from uncaged hens that are allowed free range of their houses and access to outdoor spaces. They are fed an organic diet produced according to \[National Organic Program\] [standards](https://www.ams.usda.gov/rules-regulations/organic/labeling).” An updated rule on these standards was announced in January 2017, which includes new specifics such as “daily access to outdoor areas” and “ [amount of space required indoors](https://www.federalregister.gov/documents/2017/01/19/2017-00888/national-organic-program-nop-organic-livestock-and-poultry-practices).”

Labels may also feature designations unrelated to animal welfare, such as:

- _Vegetarian-fed_ – Chickens are given vegetarian feed of corn and soy to reduce the risk of potential diseases from poultry or animal by-product feed. This creates some controversy as chickens are naturally omnivores, consuming seeds, grass, worms, insects, frogs, and snakes. Vegetarian-fed chickens can develop nutrient deficiencies if the feed is not fortified with the missing nutrients. There is also no evidence that a vegetarian diet produces more nutritious eggs than using a standard feed.
- _Omega-3 enriched_ – Eggs from chickens that are fed a diet containing ingredients like flaxseed or fish oils, which is rich in alpha-linolenic acid (ALA), a type of omega-3 fatty acid.
- _Pasteurized_ – Eggs that have been heat-treated to reduce the risk of food-borne illness such as caused by Salmonella bacteria.
- _Natural or Farm-Fresh_ – These terms are ambiguous and vary highly in meaning. They do not ensure that the chickens are raised a certain way or are fed a particular diet.

## **Prepare and Serve**

- ![Hardboiled eggs on a salad of tomatoes, greens, and olives](https://nutritionsource.hsph.harvard.edu/wp-content/uploads/2012/09/FPG_06-EggsSalad-300x200.jpg)Hardboiled: (Avoid overcooking hardboiled eggs, as evidenced by a green ring around the yolk or egg whites seeping out of the shell.) Place desired amount of eggs in a single layer in a pot. Fill with enough water to cover eggs completely. Bring water to a gentle rolling boil and allow to cook for 1 minute. Turn off heat, cover the pot, and allow eggs to sit in the water for 10-12 minutes. Remove from pot and rinse eggs with cool water. Peel and serve, or store in refrigerator for up to one week.

  - Add chopped hardboiled eggs to salads or eat whole as a snack.
  - Mash 1/4 of an avocado on a slice of whole grain toast. Top with hardboiled egg slices and a sprinkle of black pepper or red pepper flakes.
  - Replace the mayo in an egg salad with a similar quantity of hummus or mashed avocado with a squeeze of lemon and pinch of salt.
- Easy egg scramble: Heat 2 teaspoons olive oil in a frying pan on low-medium heat. Add 1 cup finely chopped vegetables of choice (e.g., tomato, bell pepper, onion, garlic, broccoli, baby spinach or arugula, mushroom, zucchini) and stir until vegetables are slightly wilted. Add 2 beaten eggs (or 1 whole egg and 2 egg whites) to mixture and stir gently until eggs solidify. Add a pinch of salt and pepper or a sprinkle of mozzarella or Parmesan cheese.
- Egg “muffins:” In a medium bowl, beat 12 whole eggs (or desired equivalent in egg whites; 1 whole egg = 2 egg whites) and set aside. Heat 1-2 teaspoons olive oil in a fry pan on medium heat and stir-fry 1 cup finely chopped vegetables of choice (see above). Divide and add cooked vegetables into a 12-cup muffin tin sprayed with nonstick spray. Pour egg mixture over the vegetables. Bake at 350°F for 15 minutes or until a toothpick inserted in the center of a muffin is clean. Allow muffins to cool in pan for a few minutes before removing. Store in refrigerator for up to 5 days or freeze for up to 1 month.

## **Did You Know?**

- While chicken eggs are the most common variety (especially in the U.S.), eggs from a range of birds—including ducks and quail—are also consumed worldwide.
- Brown eggs are not more nutritious than white. The color and size of an egg are determined by the breed of hen, which can produce white, cream, brown, blue, green or speckled eggs! The color of the yolk is also not reflective of nutritional value but the type of poultry feed.
- Eggs vary in available sizes from medium to jumbo, but eggs graded “large” are the standard size preferred in recipes.

## **Related**

- [Ask the Expert with Dr. Walter Willett: Cholesterol](https://nutritionsource.hsph.harvard.edu/2015/02/25/ask-the-expert-with-dr-walter-willett-cholesterol/)
- [5 Quick tips: Choosing healthy protein foods](https://nutritionsource.hsph.harvard.edu/2014/02/12/5-quick-tips-choosing-healthy-protein-foods/)
- [Eggs, fats, and the new dietary guidelines](https://www.hsph.harvard.edu/news/hsph-in-the-news/eggs-fats-and-the-new-dietary-guidelines/)

References

1. U.S. Department of Health and Human Services and U.S. Department of Agriculture. [_2015 – 2020 Dietary Guidelines for Americans_](https://health.gov/dietaryguidelines/2015/guidelines/chapter-1/a-closer-look-inside-healthy-eating-patterns/); 2015.
2. Hu FB, Stampfer MJ, Rimm EB, et al. A prospective study of egg consumption and risk of cardiovascular disease in men and women. [_JAMA_](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/10217054). 1999;281:1387-94.
3. Fernandez ML. Dietary cholesterol provided by eggs and plasma lipoproteins in healthy populations. [_Curr Opin Clin Nutr Metab Care_](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/16340654). 2006;9:8-12.
4. Shin JY, Xun P, Nakamura Y, He K. Egg consumption in relation to risk of cardiovascular disease and diabetes: a systematic review and meta-analysis. [_Am J Clin Nutr_](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/23676423). 2013;98:146-59.
5. Djousse L, Gaziano JM. Egg consumption and risk of heart failure in the Physicians’ Health Study. [_Circulation_](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/18195171). 2008;117:512-6.
6. Song M, Fung TT, Hu FB, et al. Association of Animal and Plant Protein Intake With All-Cause and Cause-Specific Mortality. [_JAMA Intern Med_](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/27479196). 2016;176:1453-63.

_Last reviewed August 2020_

title: Egg-Industry-Funded Research Downplays the Danger of Cholesterol (Physicians Committee)

reliability.knownStance: critical: industry-funded studies understate the cardiovascular risk of dietary cholesterol from eggs

title: Eggs: Are they good or bad for my cholesterol? (Mayo Clinic)

reliability.knownStance: moderate: most healthy people can eat eggs without materially raising cardiovascular risk

title: Are eggs risky for heart health? (Harvard Health Publishing)

reliability.knownStance: cautions that egg consumption is associated with higher cardiovascular risk in some cohorts

title: Eggs (Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health, The Nutrition Source)

reliability.knownStance: reads moderate egg intake as compatible with a healthy diet for most people

verbatimText: The foods people typically eat with eggs, such as bacon, sausage and ham, might do more to boost heart disease risk than eggs do.
Look for a sell-by date on egg cartons; eggs will generally last for four to six weeks after this date.

Research on moderate egg consumption in two large prospective cohort studies (nearly 40,000 men and over 80,000 women) found that up to one egg per day is not associated with increased heart disease risk in healthy individuals
There is also no evidence that a vegetarian diet produces more nutritious eggs than using a standard feed.
the highly refined "bad carbs" in white toast, pastries, home fries, and hash browns may also increase your risk of heart disease, stroke, and other cardiovascular diseases.
Brown eggs are not more nutritious than white.
it may be best to limit egg consumption to no more than three yolks per week
Egg whites contain no cholesterol but still contain protein.
a solid body of research shows that for most people, cholesterol in food has a smaller effect on blood levels of total cholesterol and harmful LDL cholesterol than does the mix of fats in the diet
In 2015, the U.S. Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee reported that “available evidence shows no appreciable relationship between consumption of dietary cholesterol and serum cholesterol … .”
Don’t allow cooked eggs or egg dishes to sit at room temperature for longer than two hours.
While chicken eggs are the most common variety (especially in the U.S.), eggs from a range of birds—including ducks and quail—are also consumed worldwide.
Still other research suggests that eating eggs might increase the risk of developing diabetes in the first place.
No studies reported significant net decreases in cholesterol concentrations.

Of 153 studies analyzed in the American Journal of Lifestyle Medicine report, 139 showed that eggs raise blood cholesterol (68 of these reached statistical significance, meaning the results were very unlikely to be due to chance).
A bowl of steel-cut oats with nuts and berries, for example, will be a much better choice for heart health than an egg-centric breakfast
which are only weakly related

Founded in 1985, the Physicians Committee for Responsible Medicine is a nonprofit organization that promotes preventive medicine, conducts clinical research, and encourages higher standards for ethics and effectiveness in education and research.
heart disease risk was increased among men and women with diabetes who ate one or more eggs a day
One large egg has about 186 mg of cholesterol, all of which is found in the yolk.
While eggs may be a much better choice than sugary, refined grain-based options like sweetened breakfast cereals, pancakes with syrup, muffins, or bagels
Store in refrigerator for up to 5 days or freeze for up to 1 month.
Most healthy people can eat up to seven eggs a week without increasing their risk of heart disease.
Egg dishes should reach an internal temperature of 160°F.
However, other research failed to find the same connection.

While eggs may not be the optimal breakfast choice, they are certainly not the worst, falling somewhere in the middle on the spectrum of food choice and heart disease risk. For those looking to eat a healthy diet, keeping intake of eggs moderate to low will be best for most, emphasizing plant-based protein options when possible

Cook eggs until the whites and yolks have solidified, to prevent food-borne illness.
The percentage of industry-funded studies increased over time, from 0 percent in the 1950s to 60 percent in 2010-2019.
Health experts now suggest eating as little dietary cholesterol as you can, aiming to keep intake under 300 milligrams (mg) a day.
An alarming one in three American adults has high blood pressure.
egg contains 270 international units (IU) of vitamin A and 41 IU of vitamin D. One large egg also contains about 6 g of protein and 72 calories.
It is those studies that do not find higher rates of heart attacks, strokes, or other cardiovascular diseases in people who eat up to one egg per day.

The color of the yolk is also not reflective of nutritional value but the type of poultry feed.

Overall, more than 85 percent of the studies—whether funded by industry or not—showed that eggs have unfavorable effects on blood cholesterol.

warrant: Stating the cholesterol content of an egg assumes that dietary cholesterol is a relevant factor in dietary recommendations.
defeaters: Guidelines that deem dietary cholesterol irrelevant to CVD risk

warrant: Some individual studies have reported a link between egg consumption and heart disease, challenging the notion that eggs are universally risk‑free.
defeaters: Meta‑analyses that find the overall effect null or protective

warrant: Plant‑based protein sources are associated with lower cardiovascular and overall mortality, suggesting that emphasizing them over eggs may improve health outcomes.
defeaters: Studies showing no difference in outcomes between plant protein and egg protein

warrant: Dietary cholesterol and blood cholesterol are only weakly correlated, so the cholesterol in an egg is unlikely to raise serum cholesterol enough to affect CVD risk.
defeaters: Research showing a stronger causal link between dietary and serum cholesterol

warrant: The overall fatty‑acid profile of the diet (saturated and trans fats) has a larger impact on LDL and total cholesterol than the amount of cholesterol consumed from foods such as eggs.
defeaters: Studies indicating that cholesterol intake independently drives LDL regardless of fat composition

warrant: While one egg per day appears safe, consuming substantially more eggs may increase the risk of heart failure later in life, limiting the safety claim to moderate intake.
defeaters: Evidence that higher egg intake does not raise heart‑failure risk when other dietary factors are controlled

warrant: Compared with sugary, refined‑grain breakfast options, eggs provide a healthier macronutrient profile, making them a preferable choice for heart health when consumed moderately.
defeaters: Evidence that the overall dietary pattern, not single foods, determines CVD risk

warrant: Large prospective cohort studies consistently show no association between consuming up to one egg per day and incidence of heart disease, stroke, or other cardiovascular events.
defeaters: New large‑scale studies demonstrating a positive association
Evidence that the studied populations were not representative of the general public

warrant: An average increase of about nine LDL points per day from eating an egg suggests a measurable impact on a key CVD risk factor, supporting caution for those monitoring cholesterol.
defeaters: Evidence that a nine‑point LDL rise does not translate into higher clinical events

warrant: A high proportion of industry‑funded studies, which have a higher rate of discordant conclusions, raises the possibility that reported cholesterol‑raising effects of eggs are biased.
defeaters: Analyses showing no systematic bias in industry‑funded versus independent studies

warrant: The 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee concluded that the evidence shows no appreciable relationship between dietary cholesterol intake and serum cholesterol, reinforcing the safety of moderate egg consumption.
defeaters: Later guideline revisions that adopt a more restrictive stance on dietary cholesterol

warrant: Cooking methods (e.g., frying in butter or oil) can increase the intake of saturated and trans fats, potentially accounting for higher CVD risk in some egg‑related studies.
defeaters: Research controlling for cooking method that still shows a risk

warrant: Foods commonly eaten with eggs (e.g., bacon, sausage, ham) are strong risk factors for CVD, which may confound observed associations between egg intake and disease.
defeaters: Studies that isolate egg consumption from accompanying foods and still find a risk

warrant: In people with diabetes, eating one or more eggs daily was associated with higher heart‑disease risk, indicating that the general safety claim does not apply to this subgroup.
defeaters: Studies showing no increased risk among diabetics who eat eggs

rationale: Claim [45] interprets a non‑significant cholesterol change as "there was at least a 5 % chance that the cholesterol rise could have been due to chance alone," which misrepresents the meaning of statistical significance; a p‑value >0.05 does not imply a 5 % probability of a true effect.
suggestedRemedy: Rephrase to accurately reflect the statistical result (e.g., "the observed change was not statistically significant (p > 0.05), so we cannot rule out that it was due to random variation").

rationale: Claim [4] cites the 2008 Physicians’ Health Study as supporting safety of one egg per day, but the original study population consisted mainly of male physicians, a non‑representative, health‑conscious group. The claim omits this important qualification, overstating generalizability.
suggestedRemedy: Add a qualifier noting the study’s demographic limits (e.g., "among male physicians"), or supplement with evidence from more diverse populations.

rationale: Inference [9] links an average nine‑point LDL increase from one egg per day to a "measurable impact on a key CVD risk factor, supporting caution," yet no evidence is provided that a nine‑point LDL rise translates into a clinically meaningful increase in cardiovascular events.
suggestedRemedy: Cite epidemiological data quantifying how a nine‑point LDL change affects CVD risk, or qualify the inference as a hypothesis pending outcome data.

rationale: Inference [0] claims that "Large prospective cohort studies consistently show no association..." but the only evidence cited are two cohort studies (≈40,000 men and >80,000 women). The word "consistently" extends the finding beyond the limited set of studies, creating a scope drift.
suggestedRemedy: Restrict the wording to the two cited studies (e.g., "In the two large prospective cohort studies..."), or provide additional independent studies to justify a consistent claim.

rationale: Claim [43] states that "more than 85 % of the studies, whether funded by industry or not, showed that eggs have unfavorable effects on blood cholesterol," which contradicts other presented evidence (e.g., meta‑analyses showing no effect or mixed results). The cited source does not appear to support such a high proportion of unfavorable findings.
suggestedRemedy: Verify the original source for the correct proportion, and adjust the claim to reflect the actual findings, or provide a more balanced summary of the literature.

rationale: Claim 34 specifies a weekly intake of seven eggs for diabetics, a stricter case of the daily‑egg consumption described in claim 6.
Claim 34 suggests increased heart disease risk for diabetics eating seven eggs weekly, while claim 35 reports no connection in the same population.
Claim 28 associates eggs with heart disease, whereas claim 30 emphasizes cooking method as the primary risk factor; the statements are compatible but focus on different mechanisms.

Claim 2 limits the outcome to heart disease risk, whereas claim 54 includes a broader set of cardiovascular outcomes (heart attack, stroke, etc.).
Claim 48 says most studies show eggs raise blood cholesterol, conflicting with claim 1’s weak‑relation claim.

label: Egg‑Safety Optimist
description: A perspective that emphasizes moderate egg consumption as generally safe for cardiovascular health, treating risk signals as uncertain or confounded.

label: Cautious Cholesterol Perspective
description: Emphasizes the LDL‑raising effect of dietary cholesterol from eggs and treats reassuring findings as uncertain or possibly industry‑influenced.

text: The claim rests on a single source that explicitly states “just one large egg yolk has 200 mg of cholesterol,” providing direct but uncorroborated support. No additional evidence or counter‑arguments are presented, so the claim’s current backing is limited to that lone statement.

The claim rests on two source statements that explicitly describe the relationship between dietary cholesterol and blood cholesterol as “only weakly related.” Both quotations directly affirm the claim, providing consistent textual support. No additional evidence or counter‑arguments are presented, so the support derives solely from these repeated assertions.

The claim rests on strong evidence from large prospective cohort studies that consistently find no link between eating up to one egg per day and cardiovascular events in healthy people, reinforced by the Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee’s conclusion that dietary cholesterol has little impact on serum cholesterol. Moderate‑weight arguments add that dietary cholesterol correlates only weakly with blood cholesterol and that overall dietary fat composition, as well as cooking methods and accompanying foods, play a larger role in heart‑disease risk. However, the support is narrowed by a moderate undercutting note that higher egg intake may raise heart‑failure risk, and a strong attack indicating that the safety claim does not hold for individuals with diabetes, while a weak attack cites isolated studies suggesting a possible risk.
